Hi, I'm Sidhant Gaikwad. I have around 7.5 years of experience in Quality Engineering, with a strong focus on automation, API testing, and scalable test frameworks.

Most recently, I worked at Everbridge, where I built end-to-end API automation frameworks from scratch using Python and PyTest. I also worked extensively on CI/CD pipelines using GitLab with Docker-based runners, and validated microservices deployed on Kubernetes in AWS environments.

I've also explored integrating GenAI and LLMs into testing workflows to help generate test cases and improve coverage, which significantly reduced manual effort.

Before that, at Yardi, I worked on UI automation using Selenium, TestNG, and Java, where I designed automation frameworks and helped reduce manual regression effort by around 40% through parallel execution and reusable components.

My role there was focused on validation and quality engineering — I worked on testing model outputs, handling edge cases, analyzing logs, and ensuring the system behaved reliably under different real-world scenarios. 
That experience gave me a strong foundation in testing complex, distributed systems and handling high-impact, real-time use cases.

So I built a Pytest-based API automation framework from scratch.

I started by structuring it properly — I kept test cases, API calls, and validations separate so the framework stays clean and reusable.

For authentication, I implemented a Keycloak-based token mechanism, so before executing APIs, we generate and reuse tokens instead of handling auth in every test.

I created a separate request builder class for constructing API payloads dynamically, and a data validation layer to verify responses in a structured way instead of writing assertions everywhere.

For logging, I built a dedicated logging class so we get proper logs for each step, which really helped during debugging.

I also integrated Allure reports, where I added meaningful test steps and attached request/response data, so failures were easy to analyze without going through raw logs.

I used Pytest fixtures for setup and teardown, and implemented hooks to handle logging test execution and capturing failures centrally.

To avoid duplication, I moved common operations like JSON parsing and reusable logic into utility classes, so the framework stayed clean and maintainable.

Once the framework was stable, I containerized it using Docker and integrated it with GitLab CI/CD, so tests run automatically on every deployment and can scale with parallel execution.

Overall, it made test creation faster, reduced duplication, and improved debugging and reporting significantly. Main goal was to avoid duplication and make it reusable across teams. Yeah, the reusable libraries I developed were mainly driven by repeated problems we were facing during testing.

I noticed that a lot of common logic was being rewritten again and again — like API calls, authentication handling, response parsing, logging, and validations.
This led to duplication, inconsistent implementations, and higher maintenance effort.

So instead of solving the same problem multiple times, I started creating small reusable libraries, each focused on a specific responsibility.

The idea was to centralize common functionalities so that teams could just reuse them instead of building from scratch.

Because of this:
	• Code duplication was reduced significantly 
	• Test cases became cleaner and easier to read 
	• Changes could be handled in one place instead of multiple locations 
	• And overall development and debugging became faster

The libraries were not enforced — teams adopted them because they were useful and easy to use.
We made the code available on our internal GitLab, so anyone could explore it. Along with that, we added basic documentation explaining what each library does and how to use it

API Wrapper

I built an API client to centralize request handling, auth, retry, and logging, so tests stay clean and consistent. so one of the main reusable libraries I built was the API wrapper or API client.

The reason I built it was because I noticed that in almost every test case, we were repeating the same kind of code — like calling APIs using requests, adding headers, handling authentication, 
setting timeouts, and sometimes retrying failures.

Over time, this led to a lot of duplication and inconsistency. Different people were writing API calls in slightly different ways, which made the codebase harder to maintain.
So what I did was, I created a centralized API client that acts as a single entry point for all API calls.

From a test case perspective, instead of writing the full request logic, they just call something simple like:
👉 client.post(endpoint, payload)

And everything else is handled internally.
Internally, the client does a few important things:

First, it constructs the full URL using a base URL and endpoint.
Then it builds the headers — including default headers and also authentication using eb_auth library which was again developed customly. So if a token is needed, it gets injected automatically, and the test doesn’t have to worry about it.

Then it sends the request with a defined timeout, so we avoid hanging tests.
I also added controlled retry logic — but only for cases like network failures or server-side errors. 

Along with that, I added logging of request and response, so debugging becomes much easier when something fails.

Finally, it returns the response back to the test, and the test is responsible only for validation.

The biggest benefit of this was that all the complexity of making API calls was centralized.
So if something needed to change — like authentication logic, headers, or retry behavior — we updated it in one place, and all tests automatically benefited.
Also, test cases became much cleaner and easier to read, because they were only focused on validation, not on how the API is called.

# api_client.py

import requests                 # used to make HTTP API calls
import time                     # used for retry delay handling
import logging                  # used for logging request/response details

# Create a logger instance for this module
logger = logging.getLogger(\"api_client\")
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)   # set logging level (INFO for normal visibility)


class ApiClient:
    def __init__(self, base_url, auth=None, timeout=5, retries=2):
        \"\"\"
        Constructor to initialize API client

        base_url → common base URL for all API endpoints
        auth → authentication object (from eb_auth)
        timeout → max time to wait for API response
        retries → number of retry attempts for failures
        \"\"\"
        self.base_url = base_url
        self.auth = auth
        self.timeout = timeout
        self.retries = retries

    def _build_headers(self):
        \"\"\"
        Builds request headers dynamically
        Includes default headers + auth headers
        \"\"\"
        headers = {
            \"Content-Type\": \"application/json\"   # default content type
        }

        # If auth object is provided, fetch headers from eb_auth
        if self.auth:
            headers.update(self.auth.get_headers())  
            # This is key → API client does NOT know auth logic
            # It just consumes headers from eb_auth

        return headers

    def _make_request(self, method, endpoint, payload):
        \"\"\"
        Core method responsible for making HTTP request

        method → GET / POST / PUT / DELETE
        endpoint → API endpoint path
        payload → request body (for POST/PUT)
        \"\"\"

        # Construct full URL using base_url + endpoint
        url = f\"{self.base_url}{endpoint}\"

        # Build headers (including auth if present)
        headers = self._build_headers()

        # Log request details for debugging
        logger.info(f\"Request → {method} {url}\")
        logger.info(f\"Payload → {payload}\")

        # Send HTTP request
        response = requests.request(
            method=method,       # HTTP method
            url=url,             # full URL
            json=payload,        # request body
            headers=headers,     # headers including auth
            timeout=self.timeout # timeout to avoid hanging
        )

        # Log response details
        logger.info(f\"Response Status → {response.status_code}\")
        logger.info(f\"Response Body → {response.text}\")

        # If server error (5xx), trigger retry
        if response.status_code >= 500:
            raise requests.exceptions.RequestException(\"Server error\")

        # Return response to caller (test)
        return response

    def post(self, endpoint, payload):
        \"\"\"
        Public method for POST requests
        Includes retry mechanism
        \"\"\"

        # Retry loop
        for attempt in range(1, self.retries + 1):
            try:
                # Attempt API call
                return self._make_request(\"POST\", endpoint, payload)

            except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
                # Log retry attempt
                logger.warning(f\"Retry {attempt} due to: {e}\")

                # Wait before retrying (simple backoff)
                time.sleep(1)

        # If all retries fail, raise exception
        raise Exception(\"Request failed after retries\")

    def get(self, endpoint):
        \"\"\"
        Public method for GET requests
        Same retry logic as POST
        \"\"\"

        for attempt in range(1, self.retries + 1):
            try:
                # Call GET request
                return self._make_request(\"GET\", endpoint, None)

            except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
                logger.warning(f\"Retry {attempt} due to: {e}\")
                time.sleep(1)

        # Raise exception if all retries exhausted
        raise Exception(\"Request failed after retries\")




Example Usage

from api_client import ApiClient
from eb_auth import KeycloakAuth

auth = KeycloakAuth(
    token_url=\"http://auth-server/token\",
    client_id=\"my-client\",
    client_secret=\"secret\"
)

client = ApiClient(
    base_url=\"http://api-server\",
    auth=auth,
    timeout=5,
    retries=2
)

response = client.post(\"/send\", {\"msg\": \"hello\"})

assert response.status_code == 200

I built a separate authentication library called eb_auth to solve a very common problem we were facing across teams.
What I noticed was that different teams and different products were all implementing authentication on their own — even though the underlying problem was the same.
Everyone was writing code to:
	• generate tokens 
	• attach authentication headers 
	• handle expiry 
But each team had their own way of doing it, which led to duplication and inconsistency.

So instead of letting everyone solve the same problem again and again, I created eb_auth as a centralized solution.
The idea was to make authentication simple and reusable.

I designed it in a way that all authentication types follow a common interface — basically a method like:
👉 get_headers()
Each authentication mechanism implements this method.
So for example:
	• BasicAuth handles username/password 
	• KeycloakAuth handles token generation and management 
	• JWTAuth works with existing tokens 

From a user perspective, it becomes very simple.
They just choose the authentication type they need, provide the required inputs, and call the method.
Everything else — like token generation or header formatting — is handled internally by the library.

One important thing I handled inside eb_auth was token lifecycle.
For example, in token-based authentication:
	• we generate the token 
	• cache it 
	• and automatically refresh it when it expires 
So users don't have to worry about token expiry or regeneration.

The biggest benefit was that it removed duplicated effort across teams.
Instead of each team building their own authentication logic, they could just use eb_auth.
And if a new authentication mechanism comes in, we can simply add a new class without affecting existing functionality.

So overall, eb_auth helped standardize authentication, reduce duplication, and make the system much easier to maintain and extend.


# eb_auth.py

import requests
import time


class AuthBase:
    """
    Base class for all auth types.
    Defines standard interface.
    """

    def get_headers(self):
        """
        Public method used by consumers.
        Must return headers.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError


# Basic Auth
class BasicAuth(AuthBase):
    def __init__(self, username, password):
        self.username = username
        self.password = password

    def get_headers(self):
        return {
            "Authorization": f"Basic {self.username}:{self.password}"
        }


# JWT Auth (static token)
class JWTAuth(AuthBase):
    def __init__(self, token):
        self.token = token

    def get_headers(self):
        return {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self.token}"
        }


# Keycloak Auth (token lifecycle managed)
class KeycloakAuth(AuthBase):
    def __init__(self, token_url, client_id, client_secret):
        self.token_url = token_url
        self.client_id = client_id
        self.client_secret = client_secret

        self._token = None
        self._expiry_time = 0

    def _is_token_valid(self):
        return self._token is not None and time.time() < self._expiry_time

    def _fetch_token(self):
        response = requests.post(
            self.token_url,
            data={
                "client_id": self.client_id,
                "client_secret": self.client_secret,
                "grant_type": "client_credentials"
            }
        )

        data = response.json()

        self._token = data.get("access_token")
        expires_in = data.get("expires_in", 300)

        # Add buffer to avoid edge expiry
        self._expiry_time = time.time() + expires_in - 10

    def get_headers(self):
        """
        Called externally — handles lifecycle automatically
        """
        if not self._is_token_valid():
            self._fetch_token()

        return {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self._token}"
        }


Example Usage:

from eb_auth import KeycloakAuth

auth = KeycloakAuth(
    token_url="http://auth-server/token",
    client_id="my-client",
    client_secret="secret"
)

headers = auth.get_headers()


Yeah, the main design pattern used here is the Strategy Pattern.
The idea behind Strategy Pattern is to define a common interface and allow different implementations to be swapped at runtime.
In our case:
	• the common interface is get_headers() 
	• and each authentication type — Basic, JWT, OAuth — is a different strategy 
So depending on the requirement, we just plug in the appropriate auth class, and the rest of the system works the same way.


There's also a slight use of the Template Method Pattern, especially in the improved version with a base class.
For example:
	• the base class defines the flow like checking token validity and refreshing it 
	• and child classes implement specific parts like how to fetch the token or build headers


Pseudo code

FUNCTION get_headers():

    IF auth_type is BASIC:
        RETURN {
            "Authorization": "Basic username:password"
        }


    IF auth_type is BEARER or JWT:
        RETURN {
            "Authorization": "Bearer token"
        }


    IF auth_type is API_KEY_HEADER:
        RETURN {
            header_name: api_key
        }


    IF auth_type is OAUTH / KEYCLOAK:

        # Step 1: Check if token exists and is valid
        IF token exists AND current_time < expiry_time:
            RETURN {
                "Authorization": "Bearer token"
            }

        # Step 2: Generate new token
        TRY:
            response = call token API with client_id and secret
            token = response.access_token
            expires_in = response.expires_in

            # Step 3: Store token and expiry
            expiry_time = current_time + expires_in - buffer_time

        EXCEPT:
            RAISE authentication error

        RETURN {
            "Authorization": "Bearer token"
        }


    IF auth_type is CUSTOM:
        RETURN custom_headers


# Helper logic

FUNCTION is_token_valid():
    RETURN token exists AND current_time < expiry_time


FUNCTION refresh_token():
    CALL auth server
    UPDATE token and expiry_time

Yeah, so the JSON parser library actually came out of a very practical and frustrating problem we were facing.
In our API tests, most responses were deeply nested JSONs, and almost every test needed to extract specific values from those responses.
Initially, we were using normal parsing with json.loads() and then chaining .get() calls.

So something like:
👉 response["data"]["users"][0]["id"]
or
👉 response.get("data", {}).get("users", [])[0].get("id")

The problem with this approach was:
	• It was very repetitive 
	• Hard to read and maintain 
	• Error-prone — if any key was missing, tests would fail unexpectedly 
	• And every test was re-implementing the same parsing logic 

So instead of doing this again and again, I built a reusable JSON parser library.
The idea was to simplify extraction using a query-based approach.
For that, I used the jsonpath_ng library.

So instead of writing multiple .get() calls, we could simply write:
👉 get_value(response, "$.data.users[0].id")

Internally, the parser uses JSONPath expressions to navigate the structure, which makes it:
	• much cleaner 
	• more readable 
	• and less error-prone 

The reason we didn't rely only on normal json.loads() is because it just converts JSON into a dictionary — it doesn't help with querying or extracting deeply nested values efficiently.
We would still need to write a lot of manual navigation logic on top of it.

With JSONPath, we could directly query any level of nesting in a single line, without worrying about intermediate keys.

So overall, this library helped us:
	• remove repetitive parsing code 
	• improve readability of tests 
	• and reduce failures caused by missing keys or inconsistent structures


Question
What if key is missing?
👉 JSONPath returns empty result instead of crashing — safer than chained access.

Performance?
👉 Slight overhead, but worth it for readability and maintainability in test code.

Q1: What problem did your JSON parser library solve?

Yeah, in our API tests we had to extract values from JSON responses again and again.

This created problems:

So instead of repeating this logic in every test, I created a reusable JSON parser library.

Q2: Why did you use jsonpath_ng instead of normal json parsing?
Answer:
json.loads() only converts JSON into a dictionary, but it doesn't help with extracting nested values easily.
We still had to manually navigate the structure.
With jsonpath_ng, we could directly query the JSON like this:

So it made the code:
	• cleaner

Q3: How did your JSON parser work?
Answer:
I built a wrapper function around JSONPath.
So instead of writing parsing logic in every test, we just call:
value = get_value(data, "$.data.user.id")
Internally, it uses JSONPath to extract the value and handles edge cases safely.

Q4: What edge cases did you handle?

Missing keys:
Sometimes keys were missing, which caused crashes. So instead of failing, we returned None.
value = get_value(data, "$.user.id") or None

Inconsistent structure (list vs object):
Sometimes API returned list, sometimes object. JSONPath handled both with same query.
ids = get_values(data, "$.user[*].id")

JSON inside string:
Sometimes JSON was inside a string, so we parsed it again.
details = json.loads(data["details"])

Multiple values:
Sometimes query returned multiple results.
first = get_values(data, "$.users[*].id")[0]

Null values:
We handled null safely using default values.
value = get_value(data, "$.data.id") or "default"

Q5: What was the benefit of this library?
Answer:
It reduced duplication and made test cases much cleaner.
Instead of writing complex parsing logic, tests just focused on validation.
It also reduced failures caused by missing keys or unexpected response formats.


# json_parser.py

import json
from jsonpath_ng import parse


class JsonParser:
    """
    Reusable JSON parser using jsonpath_ng
    Handles:
    - nested extraction
    - missing keys
    - multiple values
    - JSON inside string
    """

    @staticmethod
    def _parse_if_string(data):
        """
        If input is JSON string, convert to dict
        """
        if isinstance(data, str):
            try:
                return json.loads(data)
            except Exception:
                return data
        return data

    @staticmethod
    def get_values(data, expression):
        """
        Returns all matched values as list
        """
        data = JsonParser._parse_if_string(data)

        try:
            jsonpath_expr = parse(expression)
            matches = jsonpath_expr.find(data)

            return [match.value for match in matches]

        except Exception:
            return []

    @staticmethod
    def get_value(data, expression, default=None):
        """
        Returns first match or default
        """
        values = JsonParser.get_values(data, expression)

        if not values:
            return default

        return values[0]

    @staticmethod
    def get_value_safe(data, expression, default=None):
        """
        Safe version with nested JSON handling
        """
        value = JsonParser.get_value(data, expression, default)

        # If value itself is JSON string → parse again
        if isinstance(value, str):
            try:
                return json.loads(value)
            except Exception:
                return value

        return value


Example Usage:
value = JsonParser.get_value(data, "$.data.user.id")
ids = JsonParser.get_values(data, "$.users[*].id")
value = JsonParser.get_value(data, "$.missing.key", default=None)
details = JsonParser.get_value_safe(data, "$.user.details")


Pseudo code
FUNCTION extract_value(data, expression, default):

    # Step 1: Normalize input (handle top-level string)
    data = parse_if_json_string(data)

    # Step 2: Apply JSONPath
    TRY:
        matches = apply_jsonpath(data, expression)
    EXCEPT:
        RETURN default

    IF matches is empty:
        RETURN default

    value = first element from matches

    # Step 3: Handle nested JSON recursively
    RETURN resolve_nested(value)


FUNCTION parse_if_json_string(value):

    IF value is string:
        TRY:
            RETURN parse JSON(value)
        EXCEPT:
            RETURN value

    RETURN value


FUNCTION resolve_nested(value):

    # Keep resolving until no more JSON strings
    WHILE value is string:

        TRY:
            parsed = parse JSON(value)
            value = parsed
        EXCEPT:
            BREAK

    RETURN value

we were already using Allure Report, which was good for showing test results.
But the problem was, it only showed whether a test passed or failed. It didn't help us analyze data across tests.
For example, if I wanted to check response times or specific fields across multiple test runs, it was difficult.

So I built a small reporting utility.
During test execution, it collects important data like:
	• response 
	• status code 
	• timings 
And then it stores it in a structured format like JSON

So now:
👉 Allure shows test results
👉 My utility helps analyze data across tests
That made debugging and analysis much easier.

One-line: Allure is for test view, my tool is for data analysis.

Allure    - Did test pass?
My utility - What actually happened?


Why not use logger instead of your reporting tool?

we were already using logging, but the problem is logs are unstructured.
Logs are good for debugging a single test, but when you want to analyze data across multiple tests, they become hard to use.

For example, if I log something like:
Response time: 120ms
This is fine in logs, but:
	• it's not structured 
	• hard to filter 
	• hard to compare across tests 

So instead, in the reporting utility, we store data in a structured format like JSON:

{
"test": "test_get_user",
"response_time": 0.12,
"status_code": 200
}

Now this becomes very easy to:
	• analyze 
	• filter 
	• generate reports 
	• share with others


Yeah, this is a very simple utility.
During test execution, we collect important data like status code and response time using add().
All data is stored in a list.
At the end of execution, we save it into a JSON file using save().
So instead of scattered logs, we get a clean structured report.

We used a session-level fixture to create and share the report object across tests.
And we used a fixture teardown to automatically save the report after all tests complete.
So test writers only needed to call report.add(), everything else was handled automatically.


# reporting_util.py

import json
import csv
import time


class ReportUtil:
    def __init__(self):
        self.records = []

    def add(self, test_name, response, extra_data=None):
        """
        Collect structured data per test
        """
        record = {
            "test": test_name,
            "status_code": response.status_code,
            "response_time": response.elapsed.total_seconds(),
            "timestamp": time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"),
            "success": response.status_code == 200
        }

        # Optional extra data (like parsed fields)
        if extra_data:
            record.update(extra_data)

        self.records.append(record)

    def summary(self):
        """
        Return basic summary
        """
        total = len(self.records)
        passed = sum(1 for r in self.records if r["success"])
        failed = total - passed

        return {
            "total": total,
            "passed": passed,
            "failed": failed
        }

    def save_json(self, filename="report.json"):
        """
        Save full report as JSON
        """
        with open(filename, "w") as f:
            json.dump({
                "summary": self.summary(),
                "data": self.records
            }, f, indent=2)

    def save_csv(self, filename="report.csv"):
        """
        Save report as CSV (easy for Excel)
        """
        if not self.records:
            return

        keys = self.records[0].keys()

        with open(filename, "w", newline="") as f:
            writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=keys)
            writer.writeheader()
            writer.writerows(self.records)


Usage:

report = ReportUtil()

def test_get_user(api_client):
    response = api_client.get("/user")

    report.add(
        test_name="test_get_user",
        response=response,
        extra_data={"user_id": 123}
    )

    assert response.status_code == 200


def teardown_module():
    report.save_json("report.json")
    report.save_csv("report.csv")

image: python:3.10-slim

stages:
  - setup
  - test
  - report

variables:
  PIP_CACHE_DIR: "$CI_PROJECT_DIR/.cache/pip"

setup:
  stage: setup
  script:

    - pip install pytest-xdist allure-pytest

# 🔹 Parallel test execution (excluding sequential tests)
parallel_tests:

  dependencies:

        -vvv \
        -n auto \
        -m "not sequential" \
        --alluredir=allure-results
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - allure-results

# 🔹 Sequential tests (specific cases)
sequential_tests:
  stage: test
  dependencies:
    - setup
  script:
    - echo "Step 3 - Running sequential tests"
    - pytest tests/ \
        -vvv \
        -m "sequential" \
        --alluredir=allure-results
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - allure-results

# 🔹 Report stage → generate Allure report
report:
  stage: report
  dependencies:
    - parallel_tests
    - sequential_tests
  script:

At Everbridge, we were preparing for a high-capacity government client that needed to send emergency alerts to over 1 million users simultaneously. The system had a unique request ID safeguard to prevent duplicates, and manual testing showed it was working under normal conditions.

I was not satisfied with a simple pass. Under the pressure of a 1-million-user broadcast, even a millisecond of database lag could cause issues. So I engineered a concurrency test using Python's threading library to fire identical request IDs at the exact same millisecond, simulating a UI glitch or network stutter under heavy load.

My automation revealed a critical race condition. Because the backend was doing a soft check before the insert, two or more requests could pass the check simultaneously. In my tests, I triggered not just two, but up to five duplicate alerts for the same unique ID.

If that had reached production during a real emergency, it could have caused mass panic, carrier congestion, and major financial penalties. I presented my Grafana logs and automation results to the engineering director. We moved away from soft checks and implemented database-level unique constraints along with Redis-based distributed locking.

I was specifically thanked in the sprint demo for thinking like a hacker and preventing a potential PR disaster and costly infrastructure failure.

import threading
import requests
 # true_concurrency_test.py

import threading
import requests
import time
from datetime import datetime

API_URL = "https://httpbin.org/post"  # test endpoint
THREADS = 10

# Same request ID (core bug trigger)
REQUEST_ID = "REQ-12345"

# Barrier ensures all threads fire together
barrier = threading.Barrier(THREADS)


def hit_api(thread_id):
    try:
        # Wait until all threads are ready
        barrier.wait()

        # Capture exact send time
        send_time = datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S.%f")

        response = requests.post(
            API_URL,
            json={"requestId": REQUEST_ID},
            timeout=5
        )

        print(f"Thread {thread_id} | Time: {send_time} | Status: {response.status_code}")

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Thread {thread_id} failed: {e}")


def run_test():
    threads = []

    for i in range(THREADS):
        t = threading.Thread(target=hit_api, args=(i,))
        threads.append(t)
        t.start()

    for t in threads:
        t.join()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Starting TRUE concurrency test...\n")
    run_test()

So when an API request is made, the process doesn’t stop at the application layer — it goes through multiple layers before becoming an actual network packet.

First, the client sends an API request, usually over HTTP/HTTPS, which reaches the backend service. The backend processes the request, applies business logic, and prepares the data to be sent further — in our case, alert messages for 4G/5G networks.

Then this data is encoded into a protocol-specific format, where parameters like retry flags or MIME-related codes are embedded as per the requirements.

After that, the data moves down the network stack — from application layer to transport layer (TCP/UDP), and then to the network layer (IP), where it gets broken into packets.

These packets are then transmitted over the network to the target system or device.

On the receiving side, the packets are reassembled, decoded, and processed by the system.

In our case, we used Wireshark to inspect these packets and verify that the actual transmitted data matched what we configured at the API level — especially for protocol-level parameters like retry flags.

This helped ensure that the system was not just working logically, but also correctly at the network transmission level.”

🔥 If they ask “Where can things go wrong?”

Say:

“Issues can happen during encoding, where parameters may not be correctly mapped, or during transmission where packets may be altered, delayed, or dropped. That’s why packet-level validation is important.”

⚡ If they ask “Why is this important?”

Say:

“Because API success doesn’t guarantee correct network transmission — especially in telecom systems where protocol-level accuracy is critical.”

💣 If they push deeper (VERY STRONG)

Say:

“This helped us identify cases where configuration was correct at API level but not correctly reflected in the actual transmitted packets.”

🎯 One-line version

“An API request is processed, encoded into protocol-specific data, converted into network packets, transmitted, and then reassembled at the receiver — and I used Wireshark to validate this end-to-end flow.”

STAR Method: The Silent Connection Leak

Situation: At Everbridge, we released a new microservice to handle contact profile updates. During my initial automation runs, the first 100 updates passed perfectly. However, my regression suite, which ran 5,000 updates in a loop, started failing consistently after the 300th record.

Task: The developer's assessment was that it was a network fluke or an environment timeout. He did not believe it was a code bug because it worked fine for his manual sanity checks of 5 or 10 updates.

Action: I believed it was a resource leak, not a fluke. To prove it, I modified my Python script to log the database connection count from the server every 10 seconds during the test. I ran a suite of 1,000 updates and captured a Grafana screenshot showing the connection count climbing in a straight line. I then showed that connections were never being released back to the pool. Once we hit the limit of 300, every subsequent request stayed pending until it timed out.

Result: When I showed the developer the connection growth graph, he realized he had forgotten to add the .close() call, or use a proper context manager, inside the new database utility. He fixed the leak immediately. My persistence prevented a production outage that could have happened the first time a large client tried to sync their employee directory.

At Everbridge, we had a system to prevent duplicates using a unique request ID sent from the frontend. However, under high-volume automation, I discovered that the system was still triggering duplicate broadcasts for the same ID.

In automation, I used Python's threading library to send two identical API calls with the exact same request ID at the same millisecond. I did not just check the API response. I also queried the backend database or delivery logs to confirm whether two different broadcast IDs were generated for a single request ID.

That test exposed a classic check-then-insert failure. Even though the ID was the same, the system created two separate alert records. The first request was accepted, and before it could finish writing that the request ID had already been processed, the second request checked the database, saw no record yet, and started a second broadcast.

The root cause was a lack of atomicity. The code was doing a soft check with a select followed by an insert, so two threads could pass the check phase simultaneously before either one finished the insert.

My recommendation was to add a unique constraint on the request ID column so the second insert fails at the database level, and to use Redis-based distributed locking so the duplicate request can be rejected immediately.

A strong way to explain the impact is this: even with a unique ID system in place, manual testing only proves it works in low-concurrency scenarios. Automation proved that under race conditions, the safety check was still failing, which could have led to duplicate emergency notifications in production.

STAR Method: Prioritizing Critical Paths for a Major Release

Situation: At Everbridge, we had a major release scheduled for a new Visual Command Centre feature. Two days before the deadline, a last-minute hotfix was introduced to the core alerting engine. That meant I suddenly had to validate a brand-new UI feature and also perform regression on the most critical part of the system within 48 hours.

Task: My task was to ensure that the new UI did not break while also guaranteeing that the core mission of sending emergency alerts remained stable. With more than 500 automated tests and hundreds of manual scenarios, it was impossible to run everything.

Action: I took a risk-based testing approach. I first triggered a critical-path smoke suite in Python and PyTest to cover the small set of workflows responsible for most production traffic, such as login, alert creation, and delivery. I then used Git logs to see exactly which files the hotfix touched and focused my deeper manual testing on those modules. For the new UI, instead of manually entering dozens of address formats, I wrote a quick data-driven script to push the test data through the API so I could spend more time on exploratory testing.

Result: Because I focused on the highest-risk areas first, I found a small but critical bug in the hotfix where international phone formats were failing. We caught it within the first four hours, hit the deadline, and released with zero priority-one issues reported by clients.

STAR Method: The Browser-Specific CSS Bug

Situation: At Everbridge, we released a critical update to the Emergency Contact form. In staging, our automated Selenium tests passed in Chrome, and my manual smoke test in Chrome also looked fine. Shortly after release, a client using Safari on a specific iPad version reported that the Submit button was hidden behind a footer, making the form unusable.

Task: As the lead QA, I had to identify why a blocker issue reached users even though the release looked clean in testing.

Action: I reproduced the issue in BrowserStack using the same iPad and Safari version. I then analyzed the gap and found that our automation suite was heavily Chrome-focused to save execution time, and our manual sanity checks also prioritized Chrome. To fix the process, I updated our Playwright or Selenium configuration to include WebKit coverage in the mandatory pull request checks.

Result: We shipped a hotfix within two hours. More importantly, I introduced a cross-browser core suite that runs on Chrome, Firefox, and Safari for every release. Since then, we have had no UI-related production escapes on non-Chrome browsers.

STAR Method: Keycloak SSO and MFA Integration under High Pressure

Situation: At Everbridge, we were migrating our authentication system to Keycloak. The final integration was merged just six hours before a global release. If the authentication flow failed, clients ranging from emergency dispatchers to government admins could have been locked out of the system.

Task: With the go/no-go meeting only four hours away, I had to provide a high-confidence sign-off on single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and session token stability across user roles.

Action: I used a layered automation strategy to compress days of testing into hours. Instead of relying only on UI flows, I used a Python and PyTest script to hit the Keycloak token endpoint directly and simulate 50 concurrent direct-grant requests to verify JWT issuance under load. To automate MFA, I partnered with developers to use a test-only TOTP secret, and my automation used pyotp to generate valid six-digit codes dynamically. I also focused UI checks on redirect URI handling across desktop and mobile, which helped catch a configuration issue where mobile deep-link redirects were being rejected.

Result: We identified the redirect URI mismatch within the first 90 minutes, the team pushed a configuration fix before the go/no-go meeting, and the release went smoothly. I presented Grafana results showing a 100 percent token success rate and roughly 200 millisecond average authentication time, and I received a spot award for helping prevent a global authentication failure.

Yes, Pytest already provides assertions, but the problem was that it stops execution at the first failure.

In API testing, we usually validate multiple fields in a single response, so if the first assertion fails, we lose visibility on other issues.

So I built a soft assertion wrapper where we can collect all validation failures and report them together at the end of the test.

This helped in better debugging because instead of fixing one issue at a time, we could see all failures in one go.

I implemented it as a small utility class. Each check stores the failure message in a list instead of raising immediately. At the end, assert_all() raises a single AssertionError with all collected failures if any exist.

In my previous role, I worked on building an automation framework using Java, Selenium, and TestNG.

At that time, a lot of regression testing was being done manually, which was time-consuming and repetitive. So I took the initiative to design a framework where we could automate most of these test cases.

id: How did you enable parallel test execution
answer:
We used TestNG's built-in parallel execution feature.

In the TestNG XML file, we configured parallel execution at the test level and defined the thread count. This allowed multiple test cases to run simultaneously instead of sequentially.

Since our framework was designed to be independent — meaning tests didn't share state — we were able to safely run them in parallel.

This significantly reduced execution time, especially for regression suites.

<suite name="Suite" parallel="tests" thread-count="3">
    <test name="Test1">
        <classes>
            <class name="TestClass1"/>
        </classes>
    </test>
</suite>

So running tests on all browsers at the same time is achieved using parallel execution with multiple threads.

In TestNG, we define multiple configurations in the XML — for example, one for Chrome, one for Firefox — and enable parallel execution.

Each configuration runs in a separate thread, and each thread initializes its own browser instance.

So effectively, different threads are running the same tests on different browsers at the same time.

<suite name="Suite" parallel="tests" thread-count="2">

    <test name="ChromeTest">
        <parameter name="browser" value="chrome"/>
        <classes>
            <class name="TestClass"/>
        </classes>
    </test>

    <test name="FirefoxTest">
        <parameter name="browser" value="firefox"/>
        <classes>
            <class name="TestClass"/>
        </classes>
    </test>

</suite>

id: Implement a simple log aggregator. Input: a stream (or list) of log entries with fields: test_name, status (PASS/FAIL), duration_ms_component. Write a code to: Compute total tests, pass rate, and average duration per component. Return top N slowest test across all components. Explain the choice of data structures and how you'd extend this.
answer:
This solution uses a single-pass streaming aggregation technique combined with a min-heap (Top-K pattern).
We process each log once, updating counters for totals and durations, which keeps time complexity linear.
For the top N slowest tests, instead of sorting everything, we maintain a fixed-size min-heap—so we only keep the N largest durations efficiently.
From a design perspective, this follows a Streaming Aggregator pattern (incremental stats) + Top-K selection pattern using heap.
In practice, this is scalable for large log streams; one limitation is it doesn't store full history, so deeper analysis needs additional storage.

# import modules used
import heapq  # heapq gives us a lightweight min-heap for top-N selection

def aggregate_logs(logs, top_n=5):  # main function to aggregate a stream/list of log entries
    total = 0  # count total tests
    pass_count = 0  # count passed tests
    duration_sum = 0  # sum durations to compute average
    heap = []  # min-heap of (duration_ms, test_name, component) limited to top_n size

    for entry in logs:  # iterate once over the incoming stream/list
        # extract fields with minimal validation; skip malformed entries
        test_name = entry.get("test_name")  # read test name
        status = entry.get("status")  # read status
        duration = entry.get("duration_ms")  # read duration in ms
        component = entry.get("component")  # read component

        if test_name is None or status is None or duration is None or component is None:  # ensure required fields
            continue  # skip bad record rather than crash

        try:
            d = float(duration)  # coerce duration to float for safety
        except (TypeError, ValueError):  # handle non-numeric durations
            continue  # skip bad record

        total += 1  # increment total counter
        if str(status).upper() == "PASS":  # case-insensitive check for PASS
            pass_count += 1  # increment pass counter
        duration_sum += d  # accumulate total duration

        if top_n > 0:  # only maintain heap if top_n requested
            item = (d, str(test_name), str(component))  # build heap item
            if len(heap) < top_n:  # heap not full yet
                heapq.heappush(heap, item)  # push directly
            else:
                if d > heap[0][0]:  # only keep if slower than current min
                    heapq.heapreplace(heap, item)  # replace min with this slower test

    # compute aggregates with safe zero-division handling
    pass_rate = (pass_count / total) if total else 0.0  # pass rate in [0,1]
    avg_duration = (duration_sum / total) if total else 0.0  # average duration in ms

    # extract top slowest in descending order of duration
    top_slowest = []  # prepare output list
    for d, name, comp in sorted(heap, key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True):  # sort heap largest-first
        top_slowest.append({  # append a friendly dict per entry
            "test_name": name,  # test name field
            "component": comp,  # component field
            "duration_ms": d,  # duration field
        })  # end append

    # return the final aggregated result
    return {
        "total_tests": total,  # total number of processed tests
        "pass_rate": pass_rate,  # fraction of tests that passed
        "avg_duration_ms": avg_duration,  # average duration across all tests
        "top_slowest": top_slowest,  # list of the top-N slowest tests across all components
    }  # end return

# --- Example usage ---
if __name__ == "__main__":  # simple demo when running the script
    logs = [  # example list; in production this could be any iterable/stream
        {"test_name": "t1", "status": "PASS", "duration_ms": 100, "component": "auth"},  # fast pass
        {"test_name": "t2", "status": "FAIL", "duration_ms": 300, "component": "db"},  # slower fail
        {"test_name": "t3", "status": "PASS", "duration_ms": 200, "component": "ui"},  # medium pass
        {"test_name": "t4", "status": "PASS", "duration_ms": 400, "component": "db"},  # slowest pass
    ]  # end logs
    result = aggregate_logs(logs, top_n=3)  # compute aggregates and top-2 slowest
    print(result)  # print result dict

    for key, values in result.items():
        print(key, values)

import heapq  

# defining class to encapsulate flaky detection logic and maintain state
class FlakyDetector:

    # initializing detector with thresholds and limits
    def __init__(self, retry_threshold, quarantine_threshold, top_n):
        self.retry_threshold = retry_threshold  # storing retry threshold for flaky detection window
        self.quarantine_threshold = quarantine_threshold  # storing threshold for quarantine decision
        self.top_n = top_n  # storing max number of top flaky tests to track

        self.test_state_map = {}  # dictionary to store per-test stats for fast lookup
        self.recent_runs_map = {}  # dictionary to store recent execution statuses for each test
        self.daily_flaky_map = {}  # dictionary to store flaky count per test per day
        self.heap = []  # min-heap to track top flaky tests efficiently

        self.total_runs = 0  # counter for total number of test executions
        self.total_pass = 0  # counter for total number of passed tests

    # processing each test execution event
    def process_event(self, event):
        test_name = event.get("test_name")  # extracting test name from event
        status = event.get("status")  # extracting status (PASS/FAIL)
        day = event.get("day")  # extracting day for time-bucket tracking

        if test_name is None or status is None or day is None:  # validating event fields
            return  # skipping invalid events to avoid runtime errors

        if test_name not in self.test_state_map:  # checking if test is seen first time
            self.test_state_map[test_name] = {  # initializing state for new test
                "total_runs": 0,  # tracking total executions for this test
                "pass_count": 0,  # tracking pass count for this test
                "flaky_count": 0,  # tracking flaky occurrences
                "is_quarantined": False  # tracking quarantine status
            }

        if test_name not in self.recent_runs_map:  # checking if recent history exists
            self.recent_runs_map[test_name] = []  # initializing empty list for recent statuses

        if test_name not in self.daily_flaky_map:  # checking if daily tracking exists
            self.daily_flaky_map[test_name] = {}  # initializing dictionary for day-wise tracking

        if day not in self.daily_flaky_map[test_name]:  # checking if day bucket exists
            self.daily_flaky_map[test_name][day] = 0  # initializing daily flaky counter

        self.total_runs += 1  # incrementing global total runs
        self.test_state_map[test_name]["total_runs"] += 1  # incrementing per-test runs

        if status.upper() == "PASS":  # checking if current run is PASS
            self.total_pass += 1  # incrementing global pass count
            self.test_state_map[test_name]["pass_count"] += 1  # incrementing per-test pass count

        self.recent_runs_map[test_name].append(status.upper())  # adding current status to history

        if len(self.recent_runs_map[test_name]) > self.retry_threshold + 1:  # ensuring window size
            self.recent_runs_map[test_name].pop(0)  # removing oldest entry to maintain window

        is_flaky = False  # default assumption that test is not flaky
        seen_fail = False  # flag to track if failure was seen before pass

        for s in self.recent_runs_map[test_name]:  # iterating through recent statuses
            if s == "FAIL":  # checking for failure
                seen_fail = True  # marking that failure occurred
            if seen_fail and s == "PASS":  # checking for fail → pass pattern
                is_flaky = True  # marking as flaky
                break  # breaking loop once pattern is detected

        if is_flaky:  # if flaky condition is satisfied
            self.test_state_map[test_name]["flaky_count"] += 1  # incrementing flaky count
            self.daily_flaky_map[test_name][day] += 1  # incrementing daily flaky count

            if self.daily_flaky_map[test_name][day] >= self.quarantine_threshold:  # checking threshold
                self.test_state_map[test_name]["is_quarantined"] = True  # marking test as quarantined

            score = self.test_state_map[test_name]["flaky_count"]  # using flaky count as score

            heapq.heappush(self.heap, (score, test_name))  # pushing test into heap

            if len(self.heap) > self.top_n:  # ensuring heap size does not exceed limit
                heapq.heappop(self.heap)  # removing smallest element

    # printing structured summary output
    def print_summary(self):
        pass_rate = self.total_pass / self.total_runs if self.total_runs else 0  # calculating pass rate

        print("Total Runs:", self.total_runs)  # printing total runs
        print("Pass Rate:", round(pass_rate, 2))  # printing pass rate

        print("\nTop Flaky Tests:")  # printing header for flaky tests
        for score, name in sorted(self.heap, reverse=True):  # sorting heap for display
            print({  # printing each flaky test entry
                "test_name": name,  # test identifier
                "flaky_score": score,  # flaky occurrences
                "quarantined": self.test_state_map[name]["is_quarantined"]  # quarantine status
            })

        print("\nTest States:")  # printing header for detailed states
        for name, state in self.test_state_map.items():  # iterating through all tests
            print(name, ":", state)  # printing test state


# creating detector with thresholds (retry=2, quarantine=2 so we can see quarantine happening)

# sample events with repeated flaky behavior to trigger quarantine

Here is a sliding window rate limiter implementation with clear comments explaining where the sliding window starts, ends, and how it works:

from collections import deque
import time

class SlidingWindowRateLimiter:
    def __init__(self, max_requests, window_size):
        self.max_requests = max_requests          # maximum allowed requests in the window
        self.window_size = window_size            # window size in seconds
        self.request_timestamps = deque()         # deque acts as the sliding window storage

    def allow_request(self):
        current_time = time.time()                # RIGHT END of window (current time)

        # ------------------- SLIDING WINDOW START -------------------
        # LEFT END of window = current_time - window_size
        # Remove timestamps that are outside this range
        while self.request_timestamps:
            oldest_request_time = self.request_timestamps[0]  # LEFTMOST = start of window

            # If request is older than allowed window → remove it
            if current_time - oldest_request_time > self.window_size:
                self.request_timestamps.popleft()  # remove old request → window slides forward
            else:
                break  # remaining timestamps are inside window
        # ------------------- SLIDING WINDOW END ---------------------

        # Now deque contains only timestamps within:
        # [current_time - window_size, current_time]

        if len(self.request_timestamps) < self.max_requests:
            self.request_timestamps.append(current_time)  # add new request
            return True
        else:
            return False


# ------------------- EXAMPLE USAGE -------------------

limiter = SlidingWindowRateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_size=5)

for i in range(6):
    if limiter.allow_request():
        print(f"Request {i+1}: Allowed")
    else:
        print(f"Request {i+1}: Blocked")

    time.sleep(1)


Explanation of how sliding window works:

1. The sliding window is a time range defined as:
   [current_time - window_size → current_time]

2. The RIGHT END of the window is always the current request time.

3. The LEFT END is dynamic and moves forward as time increases.

4. Sliding happens when we remove old timestamps using popleft().
   This ensures only recent requests remain in the window.

5. The deque itself represents the sliding window after cleanup.

Example:
If window_size = 5 seconds:
- At time = 5 → window is [0–5]
- At time = 7 → window becomes [2–7]
- Older timestamps (like 1,2) are removed

One-Line Killer Answer:
The sliding window is maintained by removing timestamps older than current_time - window_size, so the deque always represents the active time window.

Bonus (if interviewer pushes):
Right boundary is current time, left boundary moves dynamically, and deque stores only valid requests inside that window.

This problem is solved using streaming file processing to handle very large files (e.g., 10GB) without loading everything into memory. We read the file line-by-line, filter lines containing 'ERROR', and store frequencies using a dictionary (hash map). This ensures O(n) time complexity and memory usage proportional only to unique error types.

PSEUDOCODE:

Step 2: Initialize empty dictionary error_count_map
Step 3: For each line in file:
  Step 3a: If 'ERROR' in line:
    Step 3a-i: Extract error message

Step 4: Return dictionary

# defining function to count error frequencies using streaming approach
def count_error_logs(file_path):
    error_count_map = {}  # dictionary to store error frequencies

    # opening file in read mode to process line by line instead of loading full file
    with open(file_path, \"r\") as log_file:

        # iterating over each line for memory-efficient processing
        for log_line in log_file:

            # checking if line contains ERROR keyword
            if \"ERROR\" in log_line:

                # extracting cleaned error message
                error_message = log_line.strip()

                # initializing count if error seen first time
                if error_message not in error_count_map:
                    error_count_map[error_message] = 0

                # incrementing error count
                error_count_map[error_message] += 1

    return error_count_map  # returning final frequency map

# example usage demonstrating how function works
file_path = \"sample.log\"  # path to log file
result = count_error_logs(file_path)  # calling function

# printing results
for error, count in result.items():
    print(error, \"->\", count)

    {"id": 4, "level": "ERROR", "message": "Timeout occurred"},
    {"id": 5, "level": "INFO", "message": "Request processed"},
]

# defining GET API endpoint to fetch logs with filtering and pagination
@app.get("/logs")  # endpoint path
def get_logs(
    level: str = Query(None),  # optional filter parameter for log level (ERROR/INFO)
    page: int = Query(1),      # page number for pagination (default = 1)
    size: int = Query(2)       # number of logs per page (default = 2)
):
    # ------------------- FILTERING LOGIC -------------------
    # filtering logs based on level if provided
    if level:
        filtered_logs = [log for log in logs_data if log["level"] == level]  # filtering matching logs
    else:
        filtered_logs = logs_data  # if no filter, return all logs

    # ------------------- PAGINATION LOGIC -------------------
    start = (page - 1) * size  # calculating start index based on page and size
    end = start + size         # calculating end index

    paginated_logs = filtered_logs[start:end]  # slicing logs list for pagination

    # ------------------- RESPONSE -------------------
    return {
        "total": len(filtered_logs),  # total logs after filtering
        "page": page,                # current page number
        "size": size,                # number of records per page
        "data": paginated_logs       # actual paginated data
    }

Chapter 1: The Core of RAG

1.1 What is RAG?
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a framework that improves LLM responses by giving the model access to private or up-to-date data that wasn't in its original training set. Instead of relying solely on its internal memory, the LLM "looks up" relevant documents first.

1.2 The Two Pillars
Ingestion Pipeline: Preparing your data (loading, chunking, embedding) and storing it in a vector database.
Retrieval Pipeline: Taking a user question, finding relevant chunks in the database, and sending them to the LLM to generate an answer.

1.3 What are Embeddings?
Embeddings are numerical representations of text. They convert words or sentences into a list of numbers (a vector) in a high-dimensional space where "similar" meanings are placed close together.

1.4 Popular Models
text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI): Industry standard for speed/cost.
bge-m3 (BAAI): Excellent for multilingual tasks.
all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (HuggingFace): Lightweight and runs locally.

1.5 Vector DBs
Databases designed to store and perform mathematical searches on high-dimensional vectors (e.g., Pinecone, ChromaDB, FAISS).

1.6 Code: Ingestion Pipeline

from langchain_community.document_loaders import TextLoader
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

# 1. Load: Bringing data into the app
docs = TextLoader("knowledge_base.txt").load()

# 2. Split: Breaking documents into smaller, manageable chunks
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=500, chunk_overlap=50)
chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)

# 3. Store: Embed and save to Vector DB
vector_db = Chroma.from_documents(chunks, OpenAIEmbeddings())


Chapter 2: Cosine Similarity
Cosine Similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors. It focuses on the orientation rather than magnitude. In RAG, a value closer to 1.0 means the query and the chunk are very similar in meaning.

Chapter 3: Answer Generation
Once the system retrieves the top k chunks, it creates a prompt for the LLM:
"Use the following context to answer the user question. Context: {chunks}. Question: {question}."

Chapter 4: RAG with History
Conventional RAG is stateless. To add history, we use a ChatMemoryBuffer to store the last N exchanges. When a user asks a follow-up, the LLM first "rewrites" the question based on the history, and then the system performs the retrieval.

Chapter 5: Chunking Strategies
Character: Splits at fixed character counts (simple, often cuts words).
Recursive Character: Tries to split on paragraphs, then sentences, then words (preserves context).
Document Specific: Uses format-specific loaders (e.g., Markdown header splitters).
Semantic: Uses embedding distance to detect when the topic changes and splits there.
Agentic: Uses an LLM agent to decide where a chunk logically starts and ends.

Chapter 6: Advanced Retrieval Techniques
Similarity Search: Fetches the top k vectors.
Score Threshold: Only returns results above a specific confidence level (e.g., similarity > 0.7).
Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR): MMR optimizes for both relevance and diversity. It ensures you don't get 5 chunks that say the exact same thing; it picks the most relevant, then picks others that are relevant but different from the ones already selected.
Multi-Query: The LLM generates 3-5 variations of the user's question, performs searches for all, and aggregates the results.
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): A method to merge results from different search algorithms by weighting their rank positions (ensures a result ranked high by two different methods is pushed to the top).
Hybrid Search: Combining Keyword search (BM25) (great for specific technical terms) + Semantic search (great for concepts) for the best of both worlds.
Reranking: After retrieving top 20 chunks, a specialized "Reranker" model (like Cohere Rerank) re-scores them to determine the top 5 most accurate ones, significantly improving precision.

    "What is Deep Learning?"
]

responses = llm.batch(inputs)

for r in responses:
    print(r)

5
Tools
Tools are functions or APIs that an LLM can call to perform actions
from langchain.agents import Tool

def calculator(input_text: str):
    return str(eval(input_text))

tools = [
    Tool(
        name="Calculator",
        func=calculator,
        description="Useful for math calculations"
    )
]

6: Types of messages

Messages are the structured way of passing conversation data between the user, system, and LLM."

Why Messages are needed
Instead of sending plain text like:

"What is AI?"
LangChain uses structured roles:
	• who said it 
	• what context it has 

Types of Messages
🔹 . HumanMessage
👉 input from user

from langchain.schema import HumanMessage

HumanMessage(content="What is AI?")

🔹. AIMessage
👉 response from model

from langchain.schema import AIMessage

AIMessage(content="AI stands for Artificial Intelligence")

🔹. SystemMessage
👉 sets behavior / instructions

from langchain.schema import SystemMessage

SystemMessage(content="You are a helpful assistant")

Xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage

llm = ChatOpenAI()

messages = [
    SystemMessage(content="You are a helpful assistant"),
    HumanMessage(content="Explain AI")
]

response = llm.invoke(messages)

print(response.content)

7
Structured Output
"Structured outputs ensure the LLM returns data in a fixed format like JSON, instead of free text

"The answer is around 25 dollars depending on conditions..."
{
  "price": 25,
  "currency": "USD"
}

8
Middleware
Middleware is a layer that sits between the request and response, allowing you to process or modify them.

Summarization middleware is a layer that compresses long conversation history into a short summary so the agent can continue working without exceeding token limits.

Agent =  (
	
	Model = "gpt4o",
	Middleware = [
	summarizationMiddleware(
	Model = 'gpt4o,
	Trigger = ('message',10)
	Keep =('memory',4)
	)
	]
)

9
Guardrails
Guardrails are mechanisms used to control, validate, and restrict LLM behavior to ensure safe, correct, and reliable outputs

def guardrail(input_text):
    if "illegal" in input_text.lower():
        return "Request not allowed"
    return input_text
